retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the mechanism of action of golimumab. Evaluate the potential adverse effects of golimumab. Determine the appropriate monitoring parameters for patients receiving golimumab. Implement effective collaboration and communication among interprofessional team members and patients to improve care coordination, optimize golimumab therapy for treating inflammatory conditions, and improve patient outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatic impairment: The product label does not include specific dosage adjustments for patients with hepatic impairment.

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: No labeled contraindications have been reported.

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: Current data suggest that golimumab is present in breast milk at minimal levels. This drug is likely to undergo partial breakdown in the infant's gastrointestinal tract, resulting in minimal absorption. Given the limited data, caution is advised when administering golimumab to a woman who is breastfeeding, particularly if the infant is newborn or preterm. The British Society for Rheumatology guidelines suggest that golimumab may be compatible with breastfeeding. [15] Holding golimumab for at least 2 weeks postpartum may significantly reduce the amount of medication transferred to the nursing infant. [16] Pediatric patients: Golimumab is indicated for active psoriatic arthritis and polyarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis in patients aged 2 or older.

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Healthcare professionals benefit from updated knowledge regarding golimumab administration, making informed decisions, and optimizing dosage regimens to reduce adverse reactions. This program improves patient outcomes by providing healthcare professionals with evidence-based guidance on treating RA, PA, UC, and pcJIA with golimumab. This activity provides healthcare professionals with information regarding the latest therapeutic advances, allowing for better management of these challenging conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Renal impairment: The product label does not include specific dosage adjustments for patients with renal impairment. Recent observational studies suggest that golimumab may be considered for older patients with rheumatoid arthritis and renal impairment. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Adverse effects, including severe infections and invasive fungal infections, may occur. Treatment should not be initiated during any active infections. Patients with hepatitis B are at risk for reactivation. Administration of golimumab may lead to congestive heart failure (CHF) or exacerbate underlying CHF symptoms. Lupus-like syndrome and hypersensitivity reactions such as anaphylaxis may also occur. Worsening or new-onset demyelinating disorders have also been reported. [17] Common adverse effects associated with golimumab administration include:

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Toxicity -- Signs and Symptoms of Overdose
retrieved_docs.content: Doses up to 10 mg/kg of intravenous golimumab have been administered in a clinical study without reports of severe adverse reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Golimumab, a human monoclonal antibody, belongs to the tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α) blockers class. This medication binds to soluble and transmembrane forms of human TNF-α, preventing binding with receptors. The FDA has approved golimumab for treating conditions such as moderate to severe rheumatoid arthritis (RA), psoriatic arthritis (PA), ankylosing spondylitis (AS), ulcerative colitis (UC), and polyarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis (pcJIA). This activity discusses key aspects of golimumab usage, including its mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and optimal management strategies for patients with these inflammatory conditions, particularly those unresponsive to first-line therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy considerations: Golimumab use during pregnancy may increase the risk of infections in newborns for up to 6 months after birth. Infants exposed to golimumab in utero should not receive any immunization with live vaccines for 6 months following the mother's last golimumab dose. The American College of Rheumatology (ACR) discusses the impact of placental transfer and fetal exposure for most biologic therapies, which varies with gestational stage. Biologics containing an IgG1 Fc region typically do not enter the fetal circulation until the second trimester. During the third trimester, administration of TNF inhibitors with these structural components (eg, infliximab, etanercept, adalimumab, and golimumab) results in placental transfer and notable neonatal drug levels. Limited evidence suggests no adverse effects, especially in the first trimester. The ACR guidelines recommend discontinuing these medications for the third trimester if the disease is well-controlled. However, if the disease is active, continuing TNF inhibitors to delivery is advised, though the guidelines acknowledge the likelihood of significant fetal drug exposure. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Administration -- Available Dosage Forms and Strengths
retrieved_docs.content: Golimumab is available in 50 and 100 mg prefilled syringes for subcutaneous injection and a single-dose 50 mg/4 mL (12.5 mg/mL) vial for intravenous infusion. The entire vial of golimumab should be diluted with 0.9% sodium chloride to a total volume of 100 mL. This golimumab solution can be mixed in a 100 mL infusion bag. Any solution remaining in vials should be discarded.

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Golimumab is a tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α) inhibitor targeting the human TNF-α cytokine protein and further preventing binding with its receptors. This drug is FDA-approved for treating active moderate to severe rheumatoid arthritis in sequence with first-line therapy methotrexate, active psoriatic arthritis, active ankylosing spondylitis, and patients with moderate to severe ulcerative colitis unable to achieve an adequate response to previous therapy, or patients requiring ongoing treatment with steroid. Golimumab recently received FDA approval in 2020 for its indication in the pediatric population on treating active polyarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis and psoriatic arthritis in patients aged 2 and older.

retrieved_docs.content: The initial dose of golimumab is 200 mg (subcutaneous). At week 2, 100 mg (subcutaneous), followed by 100 mg every 4 weeks

retrieved_docs.title: Golimumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Treating patients with inflammatory and autoimmune conditions requires coordination from an interprofessional healthcare team. Immediate clinical examination and diagnosis can enhance the efficiency of treatment plans, mitigating debilitating symptoms and increasing the quality of the patient's life. The interprofessional treatment team comprises a primary care physician (PCP), a rheumatologist, a gastroenterologist, nurse practitioners, physician assistants, nurses, a physical therapist (PT), and a pharmacist. Pharmacists should verify dosing, perform drug interaction checks, and report discrepancies to the prescriber. Nurses should monitor patients for adherence and clinical response.

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: To date, ten biological agents: bevacizumab, etanercept, epoetin-alfa, trastuzumab, adalimumab, pegfilgrastim, filgrastim, infliximab, rituximab, and insulin glargine, have been used as reference products for the approval of 30 biosimilar agents. Biosimilars can be used in the management of various inflammatory and autoimmune diseases. Ailments such as rheumatoid arthritis (RA), polyarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA), psoriatic arthritis (PA), ankylosing spondylitis (AS), plaque psoriasis (PsO), inflammatory bowel disease (IBD): adult Crohn disease (CD), ulcerative colitis (UC), granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) (Wegener granulomatosis), microscopic polyangiitis (MPA), and type 1 diabetes mellitus (DM). Filgrastim-sndz, a biosimilar of the reference product "filgrastim," was the first-ever biosimilar to receive FDA approval in March 2015 and is characterized as a leukocyte growth factor indicated to reduce infections caused by febrile neutropenia in the neutropenic and immunosuppressed subjects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 901
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course. This chronic course can evolve into secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease and also into chronic kidney failure (قصور كلوي مزمن). In addition, glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى) is clinically associated with chronic kidney failure (قصور كلوي مزمن). These interconnections indicate that patients with chronic inflammatory small bowel disease are at risk for metabolic complications such as secondary diabetes and subsequent renal impairment, and that glomerulonephritis may further contribute to chronic kidney dysfunction. Recognizing and monitoring these linked conditions are essential for comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 969
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course, a pattern that is also observed in secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease and in chronic kidney failure (قصور كلوي مزمن). Necrotizing glomerulonephritis represents a specific, severe form of glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى) and is closely linked to anti‑basement membrane disease (داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي), an autoimmune condition that manifests as renal dysfunction (اختلال وظيفة الكلى). In addition, necrotizing glomerulonephritis is frequently associated with pulmonary‑renal syndrome (متلازمة رئوية كلوية), reflecting its systemic impact. Glomerulonephritis, in turn, is clinically associated with the development of chronic kidney failure, underscoring the progressive nature of renal injury in these disorders. Together, these relationships highlight how chronic inflammatory processes, autoimmune kidney diseases, and metabolic complications interrelate and contribute to long‑term renal impairment, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course, indicating a prolonged and persistent disease trajectory. This chronic pattern of disease progression is similarly observed in secondary diabetes mellitus that is complicated by diabetic chronic kidney disease, where the condition also follows a chronic clinical course. The shared chronic nature of these distinct disorders underscores the importance of recognizing that patients with chronic inflammatory small bowel disease may be susceptible to metabolic disturbances such as secondary diabetes mellitus and its renal complications, necessitating vigilant monitoring and integrated management of both gastrointestinal and endocrine‑renal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2260
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course, indicating that the disease persists over time and often follows a prolonged, relapsing pattern. This chronic clinical course is also typical of secondary diabetes mellitus that occurs in the setting of diabetic chronic kidney disease, as well as of chronic kidney disease itself (قصور كلوي مزمن). The shared chronic trajectory among these conditions underscores the interconnection between persistent intestinal inflammation, secondary forms of diabetes, and progressive renal impairment. Consequently, patients with chronic inflammatory small bowel disease should be closely monitored for the development of secondary diabetes and for early signs of diabetic kidney disease, while individuals with secondary diabetes mellitus and chronic kidney disease require vigilant management of both glycemic control and renal function to mitigate further disease progression.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 882
retrieved_docs.content: The general clinical state functions as an interpretive framework for both Disease condition determination, uncontrolled and Failure (biologic function). Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, indicating that when a disease’s status is not controlled it frequently co‑exists with complicated diabetes. Failure (biologic function) is also interpreted through the general clinical state and is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ (hepatorenal failure), highlighting a link between systemic biologic failure and combined liver‑kidney failure. Recognizing that uncontrolled disease conditions and biologic function failures are reflected in the general clinical state helps clinicians assess overall patient status and guide comprehensive management.
